Las Brisas Hotel Group  ·  Palm Springs, California

THE TROPICS

A Midcentury Polynesian Resort, Reborn
92 keys· ~3 acres· $5.5M design and repositioning program· 411 E. Palm Canyon Drive

A design forward look at the project, for ownership, investors, and the creative partners who will help shape it.

The story

We are not inventing a brand. We are recovering one.

The property opened in 1964 simply as The Tropics, part of Ken Kimes' collection of Polynesian motor hotels. Its grounds were carved by the legendary Oceanic Arts, and its Congo Room steakhouse and underground Cellar Bar were a desert hideaway for Elvis and the Rat Pack. When new ownership nearly stripped the tiki soul out of it in 2001, a handful of enthusiasts threw a small fundraiser on the property to save it. That fundraiser became Tiki Oasis, which grew into the largest tiki gathering in the country before it outgrew the place and moved on.

For sixty years the building has carried a genuine legend on a motor lodge budget. Our work is simply to give the legend a building worthy of it, and to return it to its first and best name. The Tropics.

The mandate

One goal, and everything serves it.

Make The Tropics the design icon of the desert: the most awarded, most photographed, and most talked about new design hotel in Palm Springs, and a credible candidate for membership in Design Hotels. It is built for the creators, designers, and tastemakers who set the rate and tell the story. Every decision in this document serves that single goal.

What the design delivers

The product is escape.

In a stressful, noisy, polarizing world, the most valuable thing a resort can offer is permission to tune out and have fun. That feeling is not decoration. It is the product. And like a great film earning an emotion on purpose, it has to be engineered into the arrival, the pool, the bar, the light, and the quiet.

Why the numbers work

The market is the whole point.

The desert is full of five star rooms chasing the same guest, and we are happy to sit that race out. The Tropics owns a lane almost nobody serves well: the tiki and midcentury traveler who wants character, a legendary pool, and a Polynesian world to disappear into, at a rate that does not ask for a luxury budget. That is exactly why the numbers are so attractive. We are buying good bones at a good price and selling a feeling no five star can manufacture.

This is not a theory
We have spent 35 years proving it, a mile and a half up the road.

Las Brisas Hotel Group owns and operates the Best Western Plus Las Brisas Hotel in downtown Palm Springs, which TripAdvisor ranks the second best value of 142 places to stay in the city, and which carries the M.K. Guertin Award, Best Western's highest honor, earned through an unannounced annual quality inspection run in the spirit of a Michelin visit. Running a successful hotel a mile and a half from this site means we know this exact market, and this exact guest, as well as anyone in the valley. Right now we are mid renovation on a full room overhaul at Las Brisas, rooms that had gone untouched for more than a decade, so the rate we underwrite here is grounded in what we already deliver, not in a spreadsheet. Three and a half decades have taught us the quiet thing this whole plan rests on: the richest margins in the desert are not at the top of the rate card. They are in serving a specific guest beautifully, at a price that keeps the house full.

The asset

The asset earns the ambition.

The Tropics is one of the most documented tiki landmarks in the Coachella Valley and the birthplace of Tiki Oasis, a 92 key Polynesian resort at the gateway to South Palm Canyon Drive, with original Oceanic Arts carvings and a Rat Pack era history that money cannot buy. It runs today as a tired motor lodge, well below its potential and undercapitalized in both design and operations.

A disciplined $5.5M program, spent first where it moves rate, recovers it into a curated Midcentury Polynesian resort with one of the largest hotel pools in Palm Springs, over 100,000 gallons of it, and an outdoor program no boutique competitor in the valley can match.

1964
Opened as The Tropics
2001
Birthplace of Tiki Oasis
100,000+
Gallon pool
~3 acres
At the South Palm Canyon gateway
The timing

The moment fits the place.

Palm Springs sits ninety miles from Los Angeles and is the default long weekend reset for design literate Southern California. Midcentury modernism is the city's global brand, and Modernism Week is a press engine that runs every year without us lifting a finger.

The creative approach pairs a hospitality grade interior lead with a local architect of record, a tiki heritage specialist for the bar, an awards grade landscape vision for the pool and lawn, and a quiet layer of wellness and technology underneath it all. Heritage is held to a high design bar and never allowed to tip into kitsch. And the awards roadmap is sequenced honestly: local and press recognition first, the major hospitality design awards after the work is done.

What 1,600 guests already tell us

The magic is real. It just needs a setting.

Read across the property's public reviews and the same things come up again and again. These are the parts guests already love, and the parts a great design protects rather than reinvents.

The pool

The most praised thing on property and the reason people return year after year. One of the largest hotel pools in town at over 100,000 gallons, with a twelve person hot tub and mountain views.

The tiki soul

The 1964 Polynesian character, the original Oceanic Arts carvings, the Reef bar. Guests fall hard for it. This is the brand to lean into.

The grounds

Lush, tropical, and already called immaculate. The landscape is one of the property's strongest assets and carries an enormous share of the feeling.

The value and the welcome

Warm staff remembered by name, and a price that surprises people. Guests arrive low on expectation and leave planning the next trip.

The renovated rooms

When guests land one of the recently remodeled kings or king suites, or a room facing the gardens, the reviews read like a different hotel entirely. The ceiling is already proven.

The calendar

An annual tiki convention on property, Modernism Week regulars, and a loyal base that calls it home. A built in audience to design for.

The opportunity

The gap is the whole opportunity.

The honest read of the reviews is that the experience guests rave about and the room they sometimes get do not always match. That is not a story of a bad hotel. It is the natural result of a legend run on a motor lodge budget for too long, and it is exactly the gap this program closes.

The pattern is consistent: the bathrooms and the hot water, the dated finishes and tired furniture, the soundproofing between rooms, and above all the inconsistency from one room to the next. The fix is not exotic. It is a thorough, well sequenced renovation that makes every room the room people already rave about, and lets the pool, the bar, and the grounds finally sit inside a setting worthy of them.

The mood

The world we are building.

Dusk light and lagoon green, amber and bone, the original Oceanic Arts carvings, and the pool as the center of gravity. This is the visual language for The Tropics, the look we are building the whole reposition around.

The guest journey

Arrival, ritual, water.

The first thing The Tropics should give a guest is a person, not a counter, and then a path that pulls them gently from the world they came from into the one we have built. The building can choreograph the whole day from the moment they walk in.

1
The welcome
Under the original A frame entry, the check in host is the opening note. Warmth first, theatre second, the threshold into the Polynesian world.
2
The spa
A real Polynesian spa, carved from the indoor space that sits underused today, is the calm center of the property. There is no true spa here yet, only a jacuzzi, so this is a signature no competitor can copy quickly.
3
The pool
The release. The path opens onto the water, the bar, and the light. The thing guests already cross the desert for, finally framed the way it deserves.

Models worth studying for this arrival to water sequence: Two Bunch Palms organizes its whole property around the slow walk to the springs, so arrival decelerates you on purpose. The Standard Spa, Miami Beach runs its bathhouse straight into the pool so spa and water read as one continuous landscape. Amangiri anchors its plan on the spa and central pool as the gravitational center. And the Hawaiian lomi lomi tradition is the cultural reference for what a Polynesian spa can actually mean.

The grounds

Landscape does the heavy lifting.

On roughly three acres, the grounds are not a finishing touch, they are a large share of the actual product. Guests already rate the gardens among the best things here, which makes landscape one of the highest returns per dollar on the property, and it deserves an awards grade vision, not a planting plan.

A bigger move sits in the future, not in this budget: reclaiming the asphalt. The site is laid out motor lodge style, with cars parked directly in front of the rooms and wide drive aisles eating the middle. In time, pulling parking to the perimeter, tightening the aisles, and running it managed could hand real square footage back to the gardens, the cabanas, and an expanded pool deck, turning the front of every room from a parking strip into a planted lanai. That is exactly the kind of continued improvement the model is built to fund once the property proves out, and it depends on city parking rules and BIA approval, so it is an ambition we sequence for later, not a line in the opening plan.

What you are working with

The property, by the numbers.

The dimensions a design team budgets against: roughly 43,000 square feet of interior across 92 keys, on a 2.97 acre, 129,373 square foot site. The figures below are preliminary planning estimates, to confirm against the offering memorandum and an on site measure.

The number

Where the $5.5M goes.

Read by category rather than by construction phase, the budget is straightforward. More than half lives inside the 92 guestrooms, where guest perception is won or lost, and the heaviest single line is the bathrooms. A gut to studs luxury rebuild in Palm Springs runs $80,000 to $120,000 a key, and we are deliberately not doing that. The program is a refresh and elevate on intact midcentury bones, which is exactly what keeps the entry basis and the returns so attractive. The discipline is the strategy, not a shortcut.

Guestrooms · all 92
$2,914,800  $31,700 / key
Bathrooms, finishes, flooring, lighting, soft goods, casework, and in room FF&E. Bathrooms alone carry $15,000 of this per key, and FF&E carries the perception.
Public areas
$1,200,000
The Reef and tiki bar, the new spa and fitness, lobby, and pool deck.
Systems, supplies, signage
$509,000
Property technology, operating supplies, and interior and exterior signage.
Design and project management
$400,000
Architecture, interior design, and PM across concept, documentation, and construction.
Contingency · 10%
$502,380
Held for the surprises a 1964 building can offer.
Total program
$5,526,180

$2.54M
2028
Reopening
Public area completion and the details that set the tone. The Tropics opens to the world.
$200K
How the number holds

The $5.5M is the discipline, not the ceiling.

A lean program only works when every dollar is spent with intent. Here is how the budget stays honest, and where the money is protected, point by point.

Refresh, not rebuild

The $80,000 to $120,000 a key benchmark is for a full gut to studs rebuild. We are not doing that. The bones and the layouts are good, so we elevate rather than demolish, which is the entire reason the basis works.

Smart bathrooms

Where it makes sense we keep the sinks, toilets, and showers in their existing locations, so we are not rerouting plumbing and electrical. That is the single biggest cost lever on the heaviest line, and it is what we pressure test with the contractor at the property on 7/7.

Existing conditions, surveyed

One of the first things we do in due diligence is a full survey of the building, plumbing, electrical, HVAC, structural, and ADA, priced off the real property rather than guessed. The 10% contingency sits behind whatever it turns up.

Phases built for impact

Phases 1 through 4 get reworked with the architect and designer so the dollars land where they move the guest experience and the rate most, rather than spread evenly for its own sake.

Proven hands

We are mid renovation on a full room overhaul at Las Brisas right now, rooms untouched for more than a decade, in this same market. The rate we underwrite is grounded in what we already deliver a mile and a half away.

Built to keep going

Proceeds from the first several years are reinvested into the property, so the total investment in design and experience grows well past the opening number. The Tropics is built to keep improving, not to renovate once and coast.

The awards and press roadmap

Recognition, sequenced honestly.

One story engine feeds the whole thing: the recovery narrative, the Tiki Oasis origin, and the design pedigree, pushed first to local and design press, then to national travel media, timed every February to Modernism Week. The awards follow the finished work, they never lead it.

2026 to 2027
Build the story
Through design and construction. Local and design press: Palm Springs Life, Dwell, Design Milk, and a Modernism Week presence, plus trade attention through Hospitality Design as one to watch. Renderings and the narrative carry it.
Q1 2028
Launch
At reopening, target the new hotel lists: the Condé Nast Traveler Hot List and the Travel and Leisure It List, and open the candidacy for membership in Design Hotels.
2028 to 2029
Win the hardware
With the work finished and eligible: AHEAD Americas, the Gold Key Awards, the HD Awards, the Boutique Hotel Awards, and Prix Versailles, with design press through Dezeen and Architizer. Then the readers' awards that build with the reviews: Condé Nast Readers' Choice and Travel and Leisure World's Best.
Things we are still pinning down

The open questions, in the open.

A few things we want resolved off the real building and the survey before the design hardens, noted here so nothing hides.

The true key count

Listed as 92 in the sale and 90 in operation. The survey settles which, and how the suites and connecting rooms count.

Existing conditions

Plumbing, the hot water plant, electrical, ADA, and HVAC are carried in the budget and the contingency today rather than itemized. They get priced off the building before the number hardens.

The spa location

The indoor meeting room is the leading home for the spa: interior, quiet, and next to the lobby. The outdoor event lawn keeps the larger gatherings.

Parking and entitlements

How much asphalt we can reclaim for gardens and pool deck depends on city parking rules and BIA approval. That is an entitlement to win, not a given.

The pool claim

The property's own materials call it the third largest in Palm Springs. We lead with the 100,000 gallon volume, which is verifiable and impressive on its own.

The future, funded from proceeds

The bigger moves, growing the wellness program, the footprint and parking reclamation, the patios and other upside projects, are deliberately not in the opening budget. They are funded from reinvested proceeds over the first several years, only as the property proves out. The intent is to always be improving, never to spend ahead of the results.

In one line

Same bones. Same soul.

Palm Springs has plenty of what it already has. The Tropics is after the one thing it is missing: a Polynesian world with real design behind it, a legendary pool, and permission to disappear for a weekend. The legend is already here. We are giving it a building worthy of the name.

The Tropics. Recovered, not invented.
